heroOverview: Prostatocystitis is simultaneous bacterial inflammation of both the prostate gland and the urinary bladder, representing a combined infection that is more challenging to treat than infection limited to either organ alone. It typically arises when bacteria from a bladder infection ascend or spread to the prostate, or when prostatitis seeds the bladder; the two organs communicate through the prostatic urethra, so combined infection is a logical progression of untreated or undertreated lower urinary tract infection in men. The condition falls under ICD-10 code N41.3 and is classified within inflammatory diseases of the prostate. Men with prostatocystitis present with the overlapping symptoms of both conditions: urinary frequency, urgency, burning urination, perineal or pelvic pain, and sometimes fever. Because the prostate acts as a reservoir for bacteria that are sheltered from many antibiotics by its fibromuscular stroma, prostatocystitis requires longer antibiotic courses than simple cystitis — typically 4-6 weeks with a fluoroquinolone or trimethoprim.

keyStats.prevalence: Prostatitis-spectrum conditions affect approximately 8-10% of men at some point in their lifetime; prostatocystitis as a combined entity is less precisely characterised but represents a significant subset of male lower urinary tract infection (EAU, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects sexually active men aged 20-50 and older men with benign prostatic hyperplasia; immunocompromised and diabetic men are at elevated risk
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in men aged 30-50 for acute presentations; chronic prostatocystitis more common from age 40 onwards
keyStats.globalCases: Prostatitis-spectrum disease is the most common urological diagnosis in men under 50; prostatocystitis accounts for a subset where bladder involvement is confirmed

definition: Prostatocystitis (ICD-10: N41.3) refers to co-existing bacterial infection and inflammation of both the prostate gland and the urinary bladder in a male patient. It occupies a position within the spectrum of male lower urinary tract infection between isolated bacterial cystitis (N30 series) and prostatitis (N41 series). The prostate and bladder are anatomically contiguous — the prostatic urethra passes through the prostate from its base to the bladder neck — making bidirectional bacterial spread possible. In practice, most prostatocystitis cases represent a UTI in a man where organisms have seeded the prostate, or chronic prostatitis with persistent bladder colonisation. The causative organisms mirror those of male complicated UTIs: Escherichia coli predominates (approximately 75% of cases), with Klebsiella pneumoniae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterococcus faecalis, and sexually transmitted organisms such as Chlamydia trachomatis accounting for the remainder. The prostate's dense fibromuscular stroma and altered microvasculature impede antibiotic penetration, which is why the condition requires agents with good prostatic tissue penetration — specifically fluoroquinolones and trimethoprim — and longer courses than bladder infection alone. Prostatocystitis falls under the urology specialty; sexual health involvement is appropriate when an STI pathogen is identified. Without adequate treatment, prostatocystitis can progress to chronic prostatitis, prostatic abscess formation, or recurrent complicated urinary infection.

typesClassification.description: Sudden-onset combined infection of prostate and bladder with systemic signs including fever, rigors, and perineal pain; usually caused by Gram-negative bacteria and requires prompt antibiotic treatment and monitoring for abscess.

Persistent or recurrently symptomatic combined infection in which organisms persist in the prostate despite apparent treatment of bladder infection; characterised by recurrent culture-positive UTIs with the same organism in a man.
Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, or Trichomonas vaginalis infecting both the urethra/bladder and the prostate; requires STI-directed antibiotics and partner notification.

In men with benign prostatic hyperplasia, incomplete bladder emptying and residual urine provide a culture medium; organisms seeding the prostate from stagnant bladder urine produce this variant, which may recur until the BPH obstruction is treated.

primarySymptoms: Urinary frequency — passing urine far more often than normal, sometimes every 30 minutes, day and night, as both an inflamed bladder and an enlarged inflamed prostate irritate the lower urinary tract.
Urgency — a sudden, difficult-to-defer need to urinate that may result in incontinence if the toilet is not reached in time.
Dysuria — burning or stinging during urination caused by inflamed urethral and bladder mucosa.
Perineal pain or pressure — a deep aching or pressure between the scrotum and anus reflecting prostatic inflammation; often worse with prolonged sitting.
Suprapubic pain or discomfort above the pubic bone, consistent with bladder wall inflammation.
Fever and rigors when infection is acute and has provoked a systemic inflammatory response; temperatures above 38.5°C suggest bacteraemia.
Decreased or obstructed urinary flow — a weak, intermittent, or starting-and-stopping stream as the inflamed prostate compresses the prostatic urethra.
Penile tip or urethral pain, sometimes with initial haematuria, reflecting urethral involvement in the infectious process.
Ejaculatory pain or haematospermia (blood in semen) in sexually active men, as the prostate is integral to ejaculatory mechanics.
Nausea, malaise, and myalgia as systemic inflammatory markers of an acute bacterial infection involving a well-vascularised pelvic organ.
earlyWarningSigns: A urinary tract infection in a man that recurs with the same organism is a strong indicator that the prostate is harbouring bacteria — the first recurrence should prompt prostate-focused treatment.
Perineal or rectal discomfort developing during a course of antibiotics for a UTI suggests the prostate has become involved and a longer course is needed.
Lower urinary tract symptoms (frequency, urgency, weak flow) in a man that persist after a standard 7-day UTI course.
New onset of dysuria combined with rectal pressure or pain on sitting in a man with a recent sexual partner change.
emergencySigns: High fever above 39°C, rigors, and perineal pain in a man, suggesting acute bacterial prostatitis with systemic spread — requires same-day hospital assessment.
Sudden inability to urinate (acute urinary retention), which can occur when acute prostatic swelling occludes the urethra.
Signs of septic shock — rapid heart rate, low blood pressure, confusion — arising from untreated acute prostatocystitis.
A fluctuant, tender perineal mass with fever suggesting prostatic abscess requiring urgent imaging and drainage.

causes.cause: Ascending infection from the bladder via the prostatic urethra
causes.description: Bacteria colonising the bladder — most commonly uropathogenic Escherichia coli — ascend the prostatic urethra into the prostatic ducts and acini; the prostate's normal secretions are bacteriostatic but can be overwhelmed by large bacterial inoculums or when prostatic secretion is impaired, resulting in combined infection of both organs.

High-pressure voiding against bladder outlet obstruction can force urine retrograde into prostatic ducts; if that urine contains bacteria (or even sterile inflammatory mediators), it inoculates the prostatic stroma and establishes infection alongside the bladder colonisation.

Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae infect the urethra and spread proximally to the bladder and prostate; these organisms require targeted treatment distinct from the standard empirical regimens used for Gram-negative UTI.

causes.cause: Incomplete bladder emptying with stagnant urine
causes.description: Benign prostatic hyperplasia, urethral stricture, or neurogenic bladder dysfunction leaves residual urine that acts as a culture medium; bacteria multiply in stagnant urine and repeatedly seed the prostate, maintaining combined infection despite short antibiotic courses directed only at the bladder.

causes.cause: Haematogenous or lymphatic seeding of the prostate
causes.description: During bacteraemia from any source — infected skin, dental procedure, or distant infection — blood-borne bacteria can seed the prostate; simultaneous bladder seeding from urothelial bacteria explains co-infection of both organs in some immunocompromised men.

causes.cause: Instrumentation of the lower urinary tract
causes.description: Cystoscopy, transrectal prostate biopsy, and urethral catheterisation can introduce bacteria directly into the bladder and prostatic urethra, with a subsequent combined infection complicating up to 1-5% of such procedures in patients without prophylaxis.

riskFactors.description: Prostatic enlargement obstructs the bladder neck, causes incomplete emptying, and creates turbulent high-pressure voiding with intraprostatic reflux; all three mechanisms increase prostatocystitis risk and make recurrence likely until the BPH is treated.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sexual intercourse with a new partner
riskFactors.description: Sexual transmission of urethral organisms such as Chlamydia or gonorrhoea can seed both the bladder and prostate; consistent condom use with new partners reduces this route of infection.

Hyperglycaemia impairs neutrophil function, promotes organism overgrowth in urine, and causes bladder dysfunction; diabetic men have approximately 3-5 times the risk of complicated urinary infection including prostatocystitis.
Cystoscopy, transrectal prostate biopsy, and indwelling catheters introduce pathogens directly; pre-procedural antibiotic prophylaxis significantly reduces but does not eliminate this risk.

A history of any form of prostatitis or repeated male UTI indicates a prostate that has previously harboured bacteria; each episode sensitises the prostatic stroma and may disrupt secretions, facilitating recurrence.
HIV infection, organ transplant medications, or high-dose corticosteroids impair the immune response; in these patients prostatocystitis is more likely to present atypically and progress to abscess.
Narrowing of the urethra from prior infection, trauma, or surgical scarring causes high-pressure voiding with intraprostatic reflux, directly inoculating the prostate with bladder organisms.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active men aged 20-50, particularly those with new sexual partners and STI exposure
Men aged 50 and over with benign prostatic hyperplasia causing incomplete bladder emptying
Diabetic men of any age with recurrent lower urinary tract infections
Men who have undergone recent urological procedures (cystoscopy, prostate biopsy, catheterisation)
Immunocompromised men including HIV-positive individuals and transplant recipients
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing prostatocystitis requires both confirming lower urinary tract infection and demonstrating prostatic involvement. The initial assessment includes a thorough history of symptom duration, sexual history, prior UTIs and their organisms, and any recent instrumentation. A mid-stream urine sample for dipstick and culture is the first test; a positive culture in a man with perineal pain and lower urinary tract symptoms strongly suggests combined prostatic infection. Digital rectal examination reveals a tender, boggy, and sometimes warm prostate; however, vigorous prostatic massage is contraindicated in acute infection because it can provoke bacteraemia. The four-glass Meares-Stamey test, which compares bacterial counts in voided bladder fractions and expressed prostatic secretion, is the gold-standard localisation test for distinguishing prostatic from bladder infection, but is rarely performed in routine clinical practice. In practice, the combination of a culture-positive male UTI with characteristic prostatic symptoms guides diagnosis. Blood tests including full blood count and CRP quantify systemic inflammation; PSA may be markedly elevated (40-200 ng/mL) in acute prostatitis and should not be drawn or interpreted as a cancer marker during active infection. Transrectal ultrasound or CT imaging of the pelvis is indicated if a prostatic abscess is suspected — clinically suggested by failure to respond to 72 hours of antibiotics, a fluctuant tender prostate on examination, or persistent high fever. STI screening with urethral swab and urine NAAT should be offered to all sexually active men under 40 presenting with their first episode.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and its antibiotic sensitivities; a positive culture with a bacterial count of ≥10^5 CFU/mL confirms a urinary tract infection, and the same organism persisting through multiple UTIs in a man implicates the prostate as a reservoir.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a mid-stream clean-catch urine sample; results take 24-48 hours. Start antibiotics empirically while awaiting results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses prostate tenderness, size, and consistency; a warm, tender, boggy prostate supports acute prostatitis/prostatocystitis. Vigorous massage is avoided acutely due to risk of bacteraemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: With you lying on your side or standing bent forward, the doctor inserts a gloved, lubricated finger into the rectum to feel the prostate; the examination takes about 30 seconds and is briefly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (CBC, CRP, PSA, renal function)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies systemic inflammation, assesses renal function, and measures PSA; acutely elevated PSA in the context of infection does not indicate cancer and should be repeated only after 6-8 weeks post-treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results available within a few hours in an emergency or next-day in the outpatient setting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and Trichomonas vaginalis in sexually active men, as these organisms require different antibiotics and partner notification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A first-void urine sample (not mid-stream) is used for NAAT; a urethral or meatal swab may also be taken. Results typically available within 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies prostatic abscess, calcifications, or structural abnormality when clinical response to antibiotics is poor or when the prostate is unusually tender with a suspected fluctuant area.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe about 2 cm wide is gently inserted into the rectum while you lie on your side; the scan takes 10-15 minutes and is mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine flow rate and post-void residual
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bladder outlet obstruction and incomplete emptying that contribute to recurrent prostatocystitis, guiding decisions about BPH treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You urinate into a flow meter and then a quick ultrasound measures how much urine remains in the bladder; painless and takes about 15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In non-acute cases, EPS after prostate massage examined for white cells and bacteria can distinguish bacterial prostatitis (positive culture) from inflammatory non-bacterial syndrome and guide antibiotic selection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After prostate massage, a few drops of secretion are expressed from the urethral meatus and sent for microscopy and culture; performed only in the non-acute setting.

treatmentOverview: Prostatocystitis is treated with antibiotics that achieve therapeutic concentrations in both the bladder urine and the prostatic tissue, administered for longer courses than simple cystitis because the prostate's blood-tissue barrier limits antibiotic penetration. Fluoroquinolones — ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin — are the first-line agents because of their excellent oral bioavailability and high prostatic tissue penetration, achieving tissue concentrations 2-4 times higher than in serum. IDSA guidelines recommend 4-6 weeks of oral fluoroquinolone for acute bacterial prostatitis; shorter courses almost always result in relapse. Trimethoprim (or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is an alternative with reasonable prostatic penetration and is used when fluoroquinolones are contraindicated or where resistance is a concern. Beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins) penetrate prostatic tissue poorly and should be avoided as monotherapy for prostatitis even when the organism is susceptible in vitro. For STI-associated disease, doxycycline for Chlamydia or ceftriaxone plus azithromycin for gonorrhoea is used according to current STI guidelines, with treatment extended to cover prostatic penetration. Acute severe prostatocystitis with systemic signs requires hospital admission for intravenous antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem if resistant organisms are suspected) until the patient is afebrile and stable, followed by oral step-down to complete the course. Alpha-blockers such as tamsulosin reduce urethral spasm and improve urine flow during recovery and are often prescribed alongside antibiotics for symptom relief. NSAIDs provide analgesia for perineal pain. Underlying predisposing factors — BPH, urethral stricture, diabetes — must be addressed to prevent recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral fluoroquinolones are first-line for prostatocystitis; they are given for 4-6 weeks (4 weeks for most acute cases, 6-12 weeks for chronic bacterial prostatitis). Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily or levofloxacin 500 mg once daily are the standard regimens. Culture-directed narrowing is essential once sensitivities return.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological cure rates of approximately 70-90% when the organism is susceptible; relapse is common with courses shorter than 4 weeks, validating the extended duration recommendation.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative first-line agent when fluoroquinolones are contraindicated (tendinopathy risk, allergy, or significant local resistance); trimethoprim achieves good prostatic tissue levels. Used for 4-6 weeks; local resistance rates for E. coli above 20% reduce empirical use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological cure of approximately 60-80% in susceptible organisms; slightly lower than fluoroquinolones but important where fluoroquinolone resistance is a concern.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (for Chlamydia-associated disease)
medicalTreatments.description: When Chlamydia trachomatis is identified or strongly suspected (young man, new partner, urethral discharge), doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 4-6 weeks penetrates the prostate and treats both the bladder and the intracellular organism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Chlamydial prostatitis responds well with clinical cure rates above 80% at 4-6 weeks; partner notification and treatment are required.

medicalTreatments.name: Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive therapy that relaxes the smooth muscle of the prostatic urethra and bladder neck, reducing urinary hesitancy, improving flow, and decreasing intraprostatic reflux. Used alongside antibiotics rather than as standalone treatment. Started at symptom onset and continued for 4-6 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show alpha-blockers combined with antibiotics reduce symptom scores by approximately 25-35% more than antibiotics alone at 6 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs and analgesics
medicalTreatments.description: Ibuprofen or naproxen reduce prostatic and pelvic inflammation, ease perineal pain, and lower fever. They are used as symptom relief throughout the antibiotic course. In men with renal impairment or peptic ulcer disease, paracetamol is preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for pain and fever control within 24-48 hours; no direct antibacterial action but meaningfully improves quality of life during the acute phase.

surgicalOptions.name: Prostatic abscess drainage
surgicalOptions.description: When imaging confirms a prostatic abscess (most commonly by transrectal ultrasound), transurethral deroofing or transrectal aspiration under ultrasound guidance drains the pus collection. This is indicated when the patient fails to respond to 72 hours of intravenous antibiotics.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure achieved in approximately 85-95% of uncomplicated prostatic abscesses when drained promptly; untreated abscess can rupture into the rectum or perineum.

surgicalOptions.name: Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) for recurrent disease
surgicalOptions.description: In men with documented recurrent prostatocystitis driven by BPH-related incomplete emptying, TURP removes obstructing prostatic tissue, restores normal bladder emptying, and reduces intraprostatic reflux; it is a definitive solution for the obstructive substrate rather than the infection itself.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces recurrent UTI rates by approximately 60-70% in men with symptomatic BPH; performed electively after infection is fully cleared.

alternativeTreatments.name: Increased fluid intake and regular voiding
alternativeTreatments.description: Drinking 2-2.5 litres per day and voiding at regular intervals flushes the bladder, dilutes bacterial counts, and reduces residual urine volume; a simple but evidence-supported adjunct to antibiotic therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm sitz baths
alternativeTreatments.description: Sitting in a shallow warm bath for 15-20 minutes twice daily relaxes perineal and pelvic floor muscles, reduces perineal pain and urethral spasm, and improves comfort; an effective non-pharmacological adjunct particularly useful during the first week of treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelvic floor physiotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: In chronic prostatocystitis where pelvic floor tension contributes to persistent symptoms, physiotherapy-directed relaxation techniques have been shown in randomised trials to improve symptom scores more than antibiotics alone in the long term.

whySeeSpecialist: Any man with a first episode of bacterial prostatitis or prostatocystitis should be assessed by a urologist, particularly if symptoms are severe, if the presentation includes acute urinary retention, if there is clinical suspicion of a prostatic abscess, or if the infection is recurrent. The urologist evaluates for underlying structural causes (BPH, stricture, anatomical abnormality), ensures the correct antibiotic agent and duration are prescribed, arranges appropriate follow-up cultures to confirm cure, and plans treatment of predisposing anatomical conditions. STI-positive cases also require sexual health assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a urologist with an interest in male lower urinary tract infection and prostatitis management. A practice with access to urine culture, transrectal ultrasound, and uroflowmetry is important for complete evaluation. For STI-associated cases, a sexual health clinic with experience in both STI and urinary infection management is valuable. Ask about the unit's protocol for follow-up urine culture after completing the antibiotic course, which confirms bacteriological cure.
preventionStrategies: Use condoms consistently with new sexual partners to prevent STI-associated prostatocystitis.
Seek medical assessment for any urinary infection in a man — standard 7-day UTI treatments are insufficient for the prostate and set up recurrent disease.
Treat BPH or urethral stricture before it leads to recurrent infection — ask your urologist about flow testing and residual urine measurement.
Maintain good blood glucose control in diabetes, which reduces risk of complicated urinary infection.
Complete antibiotic courses fully and attend follow-up urine cultures to confirm bacteriological cure rather than stopping when symptoms resolve.
Ensure pre-procedural antibiotic prophylaxis is given before cystoscopy or prostate biopsy according to your urologist's protocol.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid prolonged sitting on hard seats during and after an episode, as direct perineal pressure exacerbates prostate symptoms — use a cushion or doughnut-shaped pillow.
Empty the bladder fully at each void; double voiding (urinating, waiting 30 seconds, then trying again) reduces residual volume.
Stay well hydrated with 2-2.5 litres of water daily to flush the urinary tract and dilute bacteria.
Avoid alcohol, caffeine, and spicy foods that irritate the bladder during treatment.
Avoid vigorous cycling and activities that create direct perineal pressure until symptoms resolve.
Complete each antibiotic course even when symptoms disappear — prostatic tissue penetration requires time and stopping early causes relapse.

recommended: Plenty of water and clear fluids to maintain dilute urine and flush the lower urinary tract
Anti-inflammatory foods — oily fish, olive oil, berries, and vegetables — to support reduction of pelvic inflammation
Moderate intake of tomato-based and quercetin-rich foods (apples, onions), which have shown modest benefit in prostatitis symptom scores in preliminary research
Probiotic foods (yoghurt, kefir) to support gut microbiome during prolonged antibiotic courses
avoid: Caffeine (coffee, tea, energy drinks) and alcohol, which irritate the bladder and worsen urgency and dysuria
Spicy foods during acute infection, which increase bladder and urethral irritability
Large amounts of red meat and saturated fat, which may promote prostatic inflammation
High-sugar drinks, particularly relevant in diabetic patients where glycaemic control determines recurrence risk

exerciseGuidelines: During acute prostatocystitis with fever and perineal pain, rest is appropriate. Once fever resolves and antibiotic treatment is underway, light walking is encouraged. Direct perineal pressure from cycling should be avoided until treatment is complete. Pelvic floor relaxation exercises under physiotherapy guidance are helpful in the recovery phase, particularly for men with chronic symptoms. Swimming and water-based exercise that avoid perineal impact are generally well tolerated once the acute phase resolves.
prognosis: With appropriate antibiotic treatment of adequate duration, the prognosis for acute prostatocystitis is good: most men achieve bacteriological cure and symptom resolution within 4-6 weeks. However, the relapse rate is higher than for simple cystitis: approximately 20-30% of men have a further episode within 12 months, particularly when predisposing factors such as BPH or urethral stricture are present. Chronic bacterial prostatitis — typically diagnosed when the same organism recurs in two or more cultures — is more difficult to eradicate and may require extended antibiotic courses of up to 12 weeks. Prostatic abscess, a rare complication affecting less than 5% of acute cases, is curable when drained promptly. The risk of infertility from prostatic duct scarring or altered seminal quality is a recognised but uncommon long-term consequence of recurrent prostatocystitis in younger men, warranting fertility counselling when this concern arises.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and acute systemic symptoms typically resolve within 48-72 hours of starting effective antibiotics. Urinary frequency, urgency, and perineal pain improve progressively over 1-2 weeks as prostatic and bladder inflammation subsides. Most men feel substantially better within 2-3 weeks, but the full antibiotic course of 4-6 weeks should be completed. A follow-up urine culture 2-4 weeks after completing treatment confirms bacteriological cure. Residual mild pelvic discomfort for 4-8 weeks after infection is common and does not indicate treatment failure.
complications: Prostatic abscess — a pus collection within the prostate requiring imaging and drainage; suggested by persistent fever despite 72 hours of intravenous antibiotics and a fluctuant tender prostate on rectal examination.

Acute urinary retention — swollen prostate tissue completely occludes the prostatic urethra, requiring emergency catheterisation; the catheter should ideally be suprapubic rather than urethral to avoid further prostatic trauma.

Chronic bacterial prostatitis — failure to eradicate organisms from the prostate leads to recurrent culture-positive UTIs with the same pathogen; a pattern of more than two episodes implicates the prostate as a bacterial reservoir.
Urosepsis — bacteraemia arising from acute prostatocystitis; elderly and immunocompromised men are at greatest risk and may present with septic shock.
Epididymo-orchitis — retrograde spread of infection from the prostate and bladder into the ejaculatory duct and vas deferens can produce scrotal pain and swelling, requiring concurrent treatment.

supportResources.name: NHS — Prostatitis
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient guide covering prostatitis types, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment, including how long antibiotic courses should last.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Prostatitis: Inflammation of the Prostate
supportResources.description: Patient-level overview of prostatitis conditions from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: Prostatitis Foundation
supportResources.description: Advocacy and education organisation for men with prostatitis, providing information on diagnosis, treatment, and peer support.

sources.title: IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Asymptomatic Bacteriuria in Adults (2019)
Nickel JC et al. Prostatitis. Canadian Urological Association Journal, 2010
Schaeffer AJ et al. Summary Consensus Statement: Diagnosis and Management of Chronic Prostatitis/Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome. Annals of Internal Medicine, 2003
Naber KG et al. Nonantibiotic treatment and targets of antibacterial strategies for prostatitis. Aktuelle Urologie, 2012
NIDDK — Prostatitis: Inflammation of the Prostate

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary tract infection (male)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A simple male UTI affects the bladder without documented prostatic involvement; prostatocystitis involves both the bladder and prostate, requiring a longer antibiotic course and a different drug class — fluoroquinolones instead of nitrofurantoin — for adequate prostatic tissue penetration.

confusedWithConditions.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: BPH produces lower urinary tract symptoms (frequency, urgency, weak flow) from mechanical obstruction without active infection; prostatocystitis adds fever, perineal tenderness, and culture-positive urine. BPH can coexist with and predispose to prostatocystitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epididymitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epididymitis presents with scrotal pain and swelling rather than perineal or suprapubic pain; it is caused by retrograde spread of urethral or prostatic organisms and can coexist with prostatocystitis, but the tender swelling is palpably in the scrotum rather than felt rectally.

relatedConditions.name: Urinary tract infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Prostatocystitis is a complicated UTI in men; the same pathogens are involved and understanding the UTI spectrum guides escalation of treatment when the prostate is involved.

relatedConditions.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: BPH is the most common anatomical predisposing condition for recurrent prostatocystitis in older men; treating the obstruction prevents recurrent infection.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Epididymitis and prostatocystitis share causative organisms and can coexist; ascending infection from the prostate can extend to the vas deferens and epididymis.

relatedConditions.name: Prostate cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Markedly elevated PSA during prostatocystitis should be repeated 6-8 weeks post-treatment; persistent elevation needs investigation to exclude prostate cancer.

faqs.question: What is prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: Prostatocystitis is a bacterial infection affecting both the prostate gland and the urinary bladder at the same time. It is more difficult to treat than a simple bladder infection because bacteria hide in the prostate's dense tissue, requiring a longer antibiotic course to clear.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are urinary frequency, urgency, and burning urination from the bladder infection, combined with perineal or rectal pressure and pain from the prostate infection. Fever, weak urinary flow, and pain during ejaculation may also be present in more severe cases.

faqs.question: How is prostatocystitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment requires antibiotics that penetrate prostatic tissue — mainly fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin, or trimethoprim. The course is 4-6 weeks, much longer than a standard bladder infection course, because the prostate's blood-tissue barrier slows antibiotic entry. Short courses almost always result in relapse.

faqs.question: Why do I need 4-6 weeks of antibiotics for prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: The prostate gland has a fibromuscular structure that limits how much antibiotic reaches the infected tissue from the bloodstream. Only certain antibiotics — fluoroquinolones and trimethoprim — penetrate well enough. Shorter courses clear the bladder but leave bacteria in the prostate, which then re-infect the bladder. Four to six weeks is the minimum needed to eradicate the prostatic reservoir.

faqs.question: Can prostatocystitis be caused by a sexually transmitted infection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chlamydia trachomatis and gonorrhoea can infect both the bladder and prostate in men. STI-associated prostatocystitis requires different antibiotics — doxycycline for Chlamydia, ceftriaxone for gonorrhoea — and partner notification and treatment. Any man under 40 with a first episode of prostatocystitis should be screened for STIs.

faqs.question: How is prostatocystitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by a positive mid-stream urine culture combined with symptoms of prostatic involvement — perineal pain, rectal discomfort, and a tender boggy prostate on rectal examination. Blood tests may show raised inflammatory markers. Transrectal ultrasound is done if an abscess is suspected.

faqs.question: Can prostatocystitis cause a high PSA?
faqs.answer: Yes, dramatically so. Acute prostatitis and prostatocystitis can raise PSA to 40-200 ng/mL, far above the levels usually associated with cancer. A PSA measured during an infection should not be used to assess cancer risk. Repeat the test 6-8 weeks after completing treatment; if it remains elevated, investigation for prostate cancer is warranted.

faqs.question: Will prostatocystitis come back?
faqs.answer: It can. Approximately 20-30% of men have a recurrence within 12 months, particularly if a predisposing cause such as an enlarged prostate (BPH) or urethral stricture is not treated. Completing the full antibiotic course and attending a follow-up urine culture to confirm bacteriological cure are the most important steps to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: Is prostatocystitis the same as prostatitis?
faqs.answer: They overlap. Prostatitis is infection or inflammation of the prostate alone; prostatocystitis means both the prostate and bladder are infected together. The treatment principles are the same — long-course fluoroquinolones — but prostatocystitis additionally has the urinary symptoms of a bladder infection alongside the perineal and pelvic symptoms of prostatitis.

faqs.question: Can an enlarged prostate cause prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: Indirectly, yes. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) causes incomplete bladder emptying, leaving stagnant urine that bacteria colonise. BPH also causes high-pressure voiding that drives infected urine back into the prostatic ducts — called intraprostatic reflux — seeding the prostate. Men with recurring prostatocystitis and BPH should have the prostate obstruction treated to break the cycle.

faqs.question: Can prostatocystitis cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Repeated episodes of prostatocystitis can scar the ejaculatory ducts and alter seminal quality, affecting fertility in some men. This is an uncommon complication but worth discussing with a urologist if you have had multiple episodes and are planning a family. A semen analysis can assess impact on sperm parameters.

faqs.question: What is the difference between prostatocystitis and a UTI in men?
faqs.answer: A male UTI treated for 7 days may seem to clear but relapse because the prostate was also infected and the short course did not penetrate it. Prostatocystitis is diagnosed when there is clear evidence of prostatic involvement — perineal tenderness, an inflamed prostate on rectal examination, or two or more UTIs caused by the same organism. It requires 4-6 weeks of a prostate-penetrating antibiotic.

faqs.question: Are there any non-antibiotic treatments for prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) reduce prostatic muscle tension, improve urine flow, and ease symptoms alongside antibiotics. Warm sitz baths relieve perineal pain. NSAIDs reduce inflammation and fever. These are adjuncts — antibiotics remain the only cure for bacterial prostatocystitis.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency room for prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: Seek same-day emergency care for high fever above 39°C with rigors and perineal pain, sudden inability to urinate, signs of sepsis (fast heart rate, confusion, low blood pressure), or if you suspect a prostatic abscess. These features require intravenous antibiotics and urgent specialist assessment rather than waiting for an outpatient appointment.

faqs.question: Can prostatocystitis spread to the testicles?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bacteria can travel from the prostate and vas deferens into the epididymis, causing epididymitis or epididymo-orchitis — infection of the epididymis and testis. This presents as scrotal pain and swelling developing alongside the urinary symptoms. If this occurs, the antibiotic course and sometimes its duration must be adjusted.

faqs.question: What foods and drinks should I avoid with prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods during treatment as they irritate the bladder and worsen urgency and dysuria. Drink 2-2.5 litres of water daily to flush the urinary tract. Anti-inflammatory foods such as oily fish and vegetables are supportive. Diabetic men should focus on glycaemic control, as high blood glucose worsens the infection.

faqs.question: How do I know when prostatocystitis is cured?
faqs.answer: Cure is confirmed by a negative urine culture performed 2-4 weeks after completing the full antibiotic course. Symptom relief alone is not sufficient evidence of cure — bacteria can persist in the prostate without causing symptoms and then re-infect the bladder weeks later. Follow-up culture is the standard of care.

faqs.question: Can women get prostatocystitis?
faqs.answer: No. Prostatocystitis by definition involves the prostate gland, which is present only in males. Women can develop bacterial cystitis and other lower urinary tract infections, but not prostatocystitis. The equivalent combined lower urinary tract infection in women would be concurrent cystitis and urethritis, which has a different clinical course and management.

faqs.question: Does prostatocystitis require surgery?
faqs.answer: Most cases resolve with antibiotics alone. Surgery is needed for a prostatic abscess (drainage), acute urinary retention (catheterisation), or recurrent disease driven by BPH obstruction (transurethral resection of the prostate). Surgery is not part of routine prostatocystitis management — it addresses complications or predisposing causes.

metaDescription: Prostatocystitis is a combined prostate and bladder infection in men needing 4-6 weeks of antibiotics. Learn symptoms, causes and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
